summary: User-journey tests simulate a typical user's complete interaction with a system to achieve a goal, functioning as a single path through a use case. As broad-stack tests, they are slow and brittle, so teams should keep suites small rather than comprehensive. These tests exercise system integration with usually just one happy-path scenario per use case, leaving behavioral variations to more focused test styles. Unlike story tests, user-journey tests aren't tied to individual user stories but are modified incrementally as stories change system behavior.
key_points: User-journey tests cover a user's entire interaction to achieve a goal, representing one path through a use case
Being broad-stack tests, they are slow and brittle, so suites should be kept small rather than comprehensive
Typically only the happy path is tested per use case, with behavioral variations verified by other test styles
User-journey tests are independent of user stories and are modified as stories change behavior rather than created anew for each story
money_quotes: User-journey tests are a form of BusinessFacingTest, designed to simulate a typical user's 'journey' through the system
Suites of user journey tests usually aren't built to be comprehensive tests of a system's behavior
Usually you will have only a few user journey tests to exercise the integration of the system as a whole - probably only one path for each use case (usually the happy path)
Verification of all the variations in behavior is left to tests done in different styles, usually with more focused coverage
When you play a story you look at the existing user journey tests and modify them to support any change in behavior implied by the user story
key_insight: User-journey tests should be few in number, covering only happy paths for system integration, while other test styles handle comprehensive behavioral verification.
